title: Model Inference & Access
summary: Call deployed Foundry models from Python with managed identity: chat completions, streaming, parameters, responses, tokens, tool calls, embeddings, async, retries, and model-specific extras.

concepts.title: What the Inference SDK Is
concepts.firstPrinciples: A deployed model is a remote function. Your code sends bytes over HTTPS, Azure authenticates the caller, the model computes tokens or vectors, and the service returns structured JSON.
concepts.definition: The azure-ai-inference package is the Azure SDK client library for model inference. ChatCompletionsClient calls chat models with messages. EmbeddingsClient calls embedding models and returns numeric vectors. Both accept Azure TokenCredential objects, so managed identity can authenticate without keys.
concepts.analogy: Think of Foundry inference like a restaurant pickup window. ChatCompletionsClient orders spoken answers. EmbeddingsClient orders coordinate maps. The deployed model is the kitchen; the endpoint is the pickup window; managed identity is your employee badge.
concepts.mentalModel: Choose the smallest client that matches the output shape: chat text from ChatCompletionsClient, semantic vectors from EmbeddingsClient. The SDK wraps REST routes such as chat completions and embeddings, but you still choose deployment names, messages, and generation settings.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  App[Python app] --> Client[Inference client]
  Client --> Auth[Managed identity token]
  Auth --> Endpoint[Foundry model endpoint]
  Endpoint --> Model[Deployed model]
  Model --> Result[Text or vector result]

code.caption: Create chat and embedding clients with managed identity only.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient, EmbeddingsClient

# Required environment variables:
# AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT: model endpoint or Azure OpenAI deployment endpoint
# AZURE_AI_EMBEDDINGS_ENDPOINT: embeddings endpoint
# In Azure hosting, prefer ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id=...) for user-assigned MI.
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

chat_client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=credential,
)

embedding_client = EmbeddingsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_EMBEDDINGS_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=credential,
)

print(type(chat_client).__name__, type(embedding_client).__name__)

drills.q: What is the model endpoint?
drills.a: The HTTPS address where your deployed model accepts inference requests.

drills.q: Why use TokenCredential instead of a key?
drills.a: Managed identity gets short-lived Entra ID tokens and avoids storing secrets.

drills.q: Which client returns vectors?
drills.a: EmbeddingsClient.

concepts.teachBack: Inference is a remote call to a deployed model. ChatCompletionsClient returns message-like text responses, while EmbeddingsClient returns numeric vectors. Managed identity gives the SDK a badge instead of a password.

glossary.definition: Running an already deployed model to produce output.
Azure SDK interface that supplies Entra ID access tokens.
The model service URL your client calls.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Getting a Client from a Foundry Project
concepts.firstPrinciples: A Foundry project groups deployments and assets. The safest inference path is to let the project client reuse the same managed-identity credential, then request an authenticated inference surface from that project.
concepts.definition: In the current azure-ai-projects docs, AIProjectClient is created with endpoint and TokenCredential. It exposes get_openai_client for an authenticated OpenAI-compatible client. The current public docs do not show project_client.inference.get_chat_completions_client; older or preview material may mention it, so treat that name as version-specific.
concepts.analogy: The project client is a building reception desk. It verifies your badge once and gives you directions to the right office. For current Foundry SDK, the documented office for model calls is get_openai_client; azure-ai-inference clients can also be created directly with the same managed identity.
concepts.mentalModel: Use AIProjectClient when your app is already organized around a Foundry project. Use project_client.get_openai_client for OpenAI-compatible Responses or Chat Completions APIs. Use azure-ai-inference directly when you want one common ChatCompletionsClient or EmbeddingsClient shape across model providers.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App as Python app
  participant Project as AIProjectClient
  participant MI as Managed identity
  participant OpenAI as OpenAI compatible client
  participant Model as Deployed model
  App->>Project: create with endpoint and credential
  Project->>MI: request token
  App->>Project: get openai client
  Project->>OpenAI: configure bearer token provider
  App->>OpenAI: create response with model deployment
  OpenAI->>Model: inference request
  Model-->>App: text response

code.caption: Current documented Foundry project client path using managed identity.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

# Required:
# AZURE_AI_PROJECT_ENDPOINT like https://account.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/project
# AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME is the deployment name shown in Foundry.
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

with AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=credential,
) as project_client:
    # Current azure-ai-projects docs expose get_openai_client.
    # If you see project_client.inference.get_chat_completions_client in older code,
    # check your installed package version and its API reference before using it.
    with project_client.get_openai_client() as openai_client:
        response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
            model=os.environ["AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"],
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello in one sentence."}],
        )
        print(response.choices[0].message.content)

drills.q: What method is documented now on AIProjectClient for an OpenAI-compatible client?
drills.a: get_openai_client.

drills.q: Is project_client.inference.get_chat_completions_client confirmed in the current public docs?
drills.a: No. It appears version-specific or older preview material, so verify your installed package.

drills.q: What credential is reused?
drills.a: The TokenCredential such as DefaultAzureCredential backed by managed identity.

concepts.teachBack: AIProjectClient represents a Foundry project. Current docs show get_openai_client, not project_client.inference.get_chat_completions_client. If samples disagree, pin the package version and follow that version's reference docs.

glossary.definition: Foundry project-level client in azure-ai-projects.
Documented method that returns an authenticated OpenAI client.
The model identifier your Foundry deployment exposes to inference calls.

sources.title: Azure AI Projects client library for Python

concepts.title: Messages and Roles
concepts.firstPrinciples: Chat models do not read your mind; they read an ordered transcript. Each item has a role so the model can separate instructions, user input, prior assistant output, and tool results.
concepts.definition: A chat completions request sends messages. The supported helper classes include SystemMessage, UserMessage, AssistantMessage, ToolMessage, and DeveloperMessage in azure.ai.inference.models. Dictionaries with role and content are also accepted.
concepts.analogy: Imagine a courtroom transcript. The judge, witness, lawyer, and clerk all speak, but the role label tells you how to interpret each line. In chat inference, system is policy, user is request, assistant is model history, and tool is external result.
concepts.mentalModel: Build messages from highest authority to newest conversation turn. Put stable behavior in SystemMessage, the learner request in UserMessage, previous model replies in AssistantMessage, and function outputs in ToolMessage only after the model asks for a tool call.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  System[System rules] --> User[User asks]
  User --> Assistant[Assistant replies]
  Assistant --> Tool[Tool result when requested]
  Tool --> Assistant2[Assistant final answer]

code.caption: Send typed messages with managed identity.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import SystemMessage, UserMessage, AssistantMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

messages = [
    SystemMessage("You are concise and explain from first principles."),
    UserMessage("What is a managed identity in one analogy?"),
    AssistantMessage("It is like an employee badge for Azure resources."),
    UserMessage("Now make the analogy even simpler."),
]

response = client.complete(messages=messages, model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"))
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

drills.q: Where do durable behavior instructions belong?
drills.a: In a system or developer message, depending on model support and your policy.

drills.q: Why include assistant messages?
drills.a: They preserve conversation history so the next answer has context.

drills.q: When do you send ToolMessage?
drills.a: After the model requests a tool call and your code has run the tool.

concepts.teachBack: A chat request is an ordered role-labeled transcript. Roles tell the model how to treat each line. Typed helper classes reduce mistakes, but dictionaries work too.

glossary.definition: Instruction message that shapes model behavior.
Message containing user input.
Message carrying a tool result back to the model.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Making a Chat Completion Request
concepts.firstPrinciples: The minimum chat call has three facts: who is calling, where the model lives, and what transcript the model should continue. The response is not magic; it is a structured object with choices, a message, finish reason, and token usage.
concepts.definition: ChatCompletionsClient.complete sends messages to the chat completions route. The model argument selects a specific model or deployment when the endpoint supports multiple models. The returned ChatCompletions object has choices, where the first choice usually contains message.content.
concepts.analogy: It is like autocomplete for a conversation. You hand the model the transcript, and it writes the next assistant line. The SDK puts that new line in response.choices[0].message.content.
concepts.mentalModel: Keep the first version boring and observable: one system message, one user message, a deployment name, and printed usage. Once that works, add parameters, streaming, tools, or JSON mode.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App as Python app
  participant SDK as ChatCompletionsClient
  participant Auth as Managed identity
  participant Service as Inference service
  participant Model as Deployed model
  App->>SDK: complete with messages
  SDK->>Auth: get bearer token
  SDK->>Service: HTTPS chat completions
  Service->>Model: run prompt
  Model-->>Service: assistant message
  Service-->>SDK: choices usage finish reason
  SDK-->>App: ChatCompletions object

code.caption: Minimal complete call and response inspection.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import SystemMessage, UserMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

response = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=[
        SystemMessage("Answer in two short bullet points."),
        UserMessage("Why do tokens matter for model inference?"),
    ],
)

choice = response.choices[0]
print("content:", choice.message.content)
print("finish_reason:", choice.finish_reason)
print("usage:", response.usage)

drills.q: Where is the generated text?
drills.a: response.choices[0].message.content.

drills.q: What does model usually mean for Azure OpenAI through Foundry?
drills.a: The deployment name.

drills.q: Why print usage early?
drills.a: It reveals token consumption, which drives limits, latency, and cost.

concepts.teachBack: A completion call asks the model to continue a transcript. The SDK returns a structured result, not just a string. Always inspect content, finish reason, and usage while learning.

glossary.definition: Candidate completions returned by the service.
Why generation stopped, such as stop sequence or token limit.
Token counts for prompt, completion, and total usage.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Controlling Output with Parameters
concepts.firstPrinciples: A model predicts next tokens with probabilities. Parameters do not change what the model knows; they change how the next-token probability distribution is sampled, constrained, or penalized.
concepts.definition: Core parameters include temperature for randomness, top_p for nucleus sampling, max_tokens for output budget, stop for stop strings, frequency_penalty for reducing repeated tokens, presence_penalty for encouraging new topics, seed for best-effort repeatability, and model for deployment selection.
concepts.analogy: Think of the model as a skilled musician improvising. Temperature controls how adventurous the solo is. max_tokens is the time limit. stop is the conductor's cutoff gesture. Penalties tell the musician not to keep repeating the same riff.
concepts.mentalModel: Change one knob at a time. For deterministic extraction, lower temperature and set max_tokens. For brainstorming, raise temperature modestly. Avoid tuning temperature and top_p together until you can explain the tradeoff.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Prompt[Messages] --> Distribution[Next token probabilities]
  Distribution --> Temp[Temperature]
  Distribution --> TopP[Top p]
  Temp --> Sample[Chosen tokens]
  TopP --> Sample
  Sample --> Limits[Max tokens and stop]
  Limits --> Answer[Final answer]

code.caption: One request showing the main generation controls.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import SystemMessage, UserMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

response = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=[
        SystemMessage("Return a practical checklist."),
        UserMessage("Checklist for calling a Foundry model safely from code."),
    ],
    temperature=0.2,          # Lower means more focused and repeatable.
    max_tokens=250,           # Caps generated output length.
    top_p=1.0,                # Usually leave at default when tuning temperature.
    stop=["END_OF_CHECKLIST"],# Stop if this text appears.
    frequency_penalty=0.0,    # Positive values reduce repeated wording.
    presence_penalty=0.0,     # Positive values encourage new topics.
    seed=1234,                # Best effort determinism, not a guarantee.
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

drills.q: Which knob caps output length?
drills.a: max_tokens.

drills.q: Should you usually change temperature and top_p together?
drills.a: No. Docs recommend not modifying both for the same request because interaction is hard to predict.

drills.q: Does seed guarantee identical output?
drills.a: No. It is best effort deterministic sampling.

concepts.teachBack: Parameters shape sampling and boundaries. Temperature and top_p affect probability selection, while max_tokens and stop bound length. Penalties and seed are useful but not substitutes for clear prompts.

glossary.definition: Sampling setting where higher values make output more random.
Nucleus sampling limit by probability mass.
Maximum generated tokens.
Best-effort deterministic sampling value.

concepts.title: JSON Mode and Structured Output
concepts.firstPrinciples: Text is flexible but brittle for machines. If downstream code needs fields, the model should be constrained to produce JSON, and your code should still parse and validate it like untrusted input.
concepts.definition: response_format controls output format. The ChatCompletionsClient reference lists 'text', 'json_object', and JsonSchemaFormat. JSON object mode asks the model to emit valid JSON text without a schema; schema mode asks it to follow a supplied schema if supported by the model.
concepts.analogy: Plain text is a paragraph. JSON mode is a form. Schema mode is a form with required boxes and field types. The model can still make mistakes, so your parser is the receiving clerk.
concepts.mentalModel: Use JSON mode for small machine-readable replies, then json.loads and check required keys. Use schema format when your model and service support it. Keep max_tokens large enough for the full JSON object.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Request[Messages plus response format] --> Model[Model constrained to JSON]
  Model --> Text[JSON string]
  Text --> Parser[json loads]
  Parser --> Validate[Check required fields]
  Validate --> App[Use typed data]

code.caption: Request JSON object output and validate it.
code.content:
import json
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import SystemMessage, UserMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

response = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    response_format="json_object",
    messages=[
        SystemMessage("Return only valid JSON with keys summary and risk."),
        UserMessage("Summarize the risk of hard-coding an API key."),
    ],
    max_tokens=200,
)

payload = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
if not {"summary", "risk"}.issubset(payload):
    raise ValueError(f"Missing expected keys: {payload}")
print(payload["summary"])

drills.q: What does json_object guarantee better than plain text?
drills.a: The model is asked to return JSON-formatted text.

drills.q: Should you skip parsing validation because JSON mode was used?
drills.a: No. Always parse and validate.

drills.q: What can JsonSchemaFormat add?
drills.a: A schema the model should follow when supported.

concepts.teachBack: JSON mode turns a prose answer into machine-readable text. It does not remove the need for validation. Use schema support where available for stricter contracts.

glossary.definition: Parameter that controls text versus JSON-shaped model output.
JSON mode without a provided schema.
Model output format constrained by a JSON schema.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Reading the Response Object
concepts.firstPrinciples: The response object is your flight recorder. It tells you what the model said, why it stopped, and how many tokens were consumed. Treat it as telemetry, not just a string container.
concepts.definition: A non-streaming complete call returns ChatCompletions. Important fields are choices, each choice's message.content, finish_reason, and response.usage. Usage reports prompt, completion, and total token counts when the service provides them.
concepts.analogy: A restaurant receipt has items, subtotal, and payment status. The model response has content, stop reason, and token bill. If the meal ended early because the kitchen closed, finish_reason helps you notice.
concepts.mentalModel: Always check finish_reason before trusting a truncated answer. Log usage at info level for capacity planning. If you request multiple choices, never assume choice zero is the only relevant one without checking your n setting and application policy.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Response[ChatCompletions] --> Choices[choices list]
  Choices --> Message[message content]
  Choices --> Finish[finish reason]
  Response --> Usage[usage tokens]
  Usage --> Prompt[prompt tokens]
  Usage --> Completion[completion tokens]
  Usage --> Total[total tokens]

code.caption: Safely inspect content, finish reason, and usage fields.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import UserMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

response = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=[UserMessage("Explain context windows in one sentence.")],
    max_tokens=80,
)

for i, choice in enumerate(response.choices):
    print(f"choice {i} finish_reason={choice.finish_reason}")
    print(choice.message.content)

if response.usage:
    print("prompt tokens:", response.usage.prompt_tokens)
    print("completion tokens:", response.usage.completion_tokens)
    print("total tokens:", response.usage.total_tokens)

drills.q: Which field tells why generation stopped?
drills.a: choice.finish_reason.

drills.q: Which field usually contains answer text?
drills.a: choice.message.content.

drills.q: Why save usage metrics?
drills.a: They explain cost, rate-limit pressure, and context-window consumption.

concepts.teachBack: Do not throw away response metadata. choices hold candidate answers, finish_reason explains stopping, and usage accounts for tokens. That metadata is how production inference becomes debuggable.

glossary.definition: Non-streaming response type for chat completions.
Tokens consumed by input messages.
Tokens generated by the model.

concepts.title: Streaming Responses
concepts.firstPrinciples: Without streaming, your user waits until the whole answer exists. With streaming, the service sends small updates as tokens are produced, reducing perceived latency and enabling progress displays.
concepts.definition: ChatCompletionsClient.complete accepts stream=True. The result is an iterable StreamingChatCompletions object. Each update can contain choices[0].delta.content, and some updates may contain usage.
concepts.analogy: A normal response is receiving a finished book. Streaming is watching the author type line by line. You still need to bind the pages together at the end if your application needs the full text.
concepts.mentalModel: Stream for chat UIs, long answers, and progress-sensitive work. Accumulate deltas into a buffer if later code needs the complete answer. Remember that not every streaming event contains content.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App as Python app
  participant SDK as ChatCompletionsClient
  participant Service as Inference service
  participant UI as User interface
  App->>SDK: complete stream true
  SDK->>Service: streaming request
  Service-->>SDK: delta one
  SDK-->>UI: print token text
  Service-->>SDK: delta two
  SDK-->>UI: append token text
  Service-->>SDK: final usage
  SDK-->>App: stream ends

code.caption: Consume streaming deltas and assemble the final answer.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import SystemMessage, UserMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

stream = client.complete(
    stream=True,
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=[
        SystemMessage("Write in short sentences."),
        UserMessage("Teach me what streaming means for LLMs."),
    ],
)

parts = []
for update in stream:
    if update.choices and update.choices[0].delta:
        text = update.choices[0].delta.content or ""
        print(text, end="", flush=True)
        parts.append(text)
    if update.usage:
        print(f"
usage: {update.usage}")

full_answer = "".join(parts)
print("
received characters:", len(full_answer))

drills.q: What flag enables streaming?
drills.a: stream=True.

drills.q: Where is streamed text usually found?
drills.a: update.choices[0].delta.content.

drills.q: Why check for missing choices or delta?
drills.a: Some stream events carry metadata or usage instead of text.

concepts.teachBack: Streaming sends partial updates instead of waiting for the final object. Read delta.content carefully and accumulate if needed. It improves perceived latency but requires event-loop style thinking.

glossary.definition: Partial message update in a streaming response.
Iterable stream of chat completion updates.
How long the user feels they waited before seeing progress.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Tokens, Context Windows, Cost, and Limits
concepts.firstPrinciples: Models process tokens, not characters or words. Every input token occupies context capacity and every output token consumes generation budget. Limits and bills follow token counts.
concepts.definition: A token is a chunk of text produced by the model's tokenizer. The context window is the maximum tokens the model can consider in one request, including messages, tool schemas, tool results, and generated output. Usage fields report token counts when available.
concepts.analogy: Tokens are like luggage weight. You can pack words, code, JSON schemas, and history, but the plane has a fixed weight limit. The ticket price and boarding rules care about total weight, not how emotionally important each item is.
concepts.mentalModel: Estimate before calling, measure after calling. Keep prompts short, summarize history, cap max_tokens, and log usage. Token counting can vary by model tokenizer, so use provider-specific tokenizers when exact counts matter.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  System[System tokens] --> Total[Total context tokens]
  User[User tokens] --> Total
  History[History tokens] --> Total
  Tools[Tool schema tokens] --> Total
  Output[Reserved output tokens] --> Total
  Total --> Window[Context window limit]
  Total --> Cost[Cost and rate limits]

code.caption: Crude estimation plus real usage measurement.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import UserMessage

# Rough estimate only. Use model-specific tokenizers for exact preflight counts.
def rough_token_estimate(text: str) -> int:
    return max(1, len(text) // 4)

prompt = "Explain why context windows matter using a backpack analogy."
print("rough prompt token estimate:", rough_token_estimate(prompt))

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

response = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=[UserMessage(prompt)],
    max_tokens=120,
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)
if response.usage:
    print("actual usage from service:", response.usage)

drills.q: Do models count words directly?
drills.a: No. They count tokens produced by tokenization.

drills.q: What consumes context window?
drills.a: Input messages, history, tool schemas, tool results, and output budget.

drills.q: What field gives post-call token counts?
drills.a: response.usage when provided.

concepts.teachBack: Tokens are the accounting unit of model inference. Context windows are finite token workspaces. Cost, latency, and rate limits all become easier to manage once you log token usage.

glossary.definition: A model-specific chunk of text processed by the model.
Maximum tokens the model can consider in one request.
Algorithm that converts text to model tokens.

concepts.title: Function Calling at the Inference Layer
concepts.firstPrinciples: The model cannot actually execute your Python function. It can only request a function call by returning structured arguments. Your code decides whether to run the function, runs it, then sends the result back as a tool message.
concepts.definition: ChatCompletionsClient supports tools and tool_choice. Tool definitions describe caller-defined functions. The model may return message.tool_calls. The caller executes the requested function and sends ToolMessage results back in a second complete call. This is the raw inference mechanic; MCP standardizes how tools are exposed, but is a separate lane.
concepts.analogy: The model is a dispatcher, not a mechanic. It writes a work order: call get_weather with city Seattle. Your code is the mechanic that performs the work and returns the result ticket.
concepts.mentalModel: Tool calling is a two-step loop: ask with tool definitions, inspect tool_calls, execute only allowed functions, then continue with assistant message plus ToolMessage. Validate JSON arguments because they came from the model.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App as Python app
  participant Model as Chat model
  participant Func as Local function
  App->>Model: messages plus tool definitions
  Model-->>App: assistant message with tool calls
  App->>Func: execute validated function
  Func-->>App: tool result JSON
  App->>Model: prior assistant tool call plus tool message
  Model-->>App: final answer

code.caption: Minimal two-call function-calling loop with validation.

drills.q: Does the model run your function directly?
drills.a: No. It returns a requested tool call; your code executes it.

drills.q: What message sends tool output back?
drills.a: ToolMessage with the matching tool_call_id.

drills.q: How is this related to MCP?
drills.a: It is the lower-level mechanism MCP can build on or expose through a standard protocol.

concepts.teachBack: Function calling is a controlled handshake. The model requests, your code validates and executes, then the model summarizes the result. Never execute arbitrary tool names or unvalidated arguments.

glossary.definition: Model-requested function invocation with name and arguments.
Schema describing a callable function to the model.
Setting controlling whether and which tool the model may call.

sources.title: Azure SDK Python tool calling sample

concepts.title: Embeddings and Vector Output
concepts.firstPrinciples: An embedding converts meaning into coordinates. Similar meanings land near each other in vector space, making search, clustering, recommendations, and retrieval possible.
concepts.definition: EmbeddingsClient calls the embeddings route and returns EmbeddingsResult. The embed method accepts input as a list of strings and can take model, dimensions, encoding_format, input_type, and model_extras. Each response item has an embedding vector and index.
concepts.analogy: If text meaning were a city, an embedding is GPS coordinates. Similar sentences are nearby neighborhoods. A vector database is the map index that finds nearby coordinates quickly.
concepts.mentalModel: Use embeddings for comparison, not for generation. Store vectors with the original text and metadata. At query time, embed the query, retrieve nearest vectors, and pass matching text to chat only if you are building retrieval-augmented generation.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Text[Input text] --> Embed[EmbeddingsClient embed]
  Embed --> Vector[Float vector]
  Vector --> Store[Vector index]
  Query[Query text] --> QueryVec[Query vector]
  QueryVec --> Search[Nearest neighbor search]
  Store --> Search
  Search --> Matches[Relevant documents]

code.caption: Call the embeddings client and inspect vector lengths.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import EmbeddingsClient

client = EmbeddingsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_EMBEDDINGS_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

response = client.embed(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_EMBEDDINGS_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    input=[
        "Managed identity removes secrets from code.",
        "API keys should not be hard-coded.",
        "Bananas ripen on the counter.",
    ],
    input_type="document",
)

for item in response.data:
    print(f"index={item.index} dimensions={len(item.embedding)} sample={item.embedding[:3]}")
if response.usage:
    print("embedding token usage:", response.usage)

drills.q: What does an embedding represent?
drills.a: Text meaning as a numeric vector.

drills.q: Are embeddings generated prose?
drills.a: No. They are numbers for similarity and retrieval.

drills.q: Why keep the original text with the vector?
drills.a: Vectors are for search; users and models need the original content.

concepts.teachBack: Embeddings are meaning coordinates. EmbeddingsClient.embed returns vectors and usage. Use vectors to find related text, then use the original text for display or chat grounding.

glossary.definition: Numeric vector representing semantic meaning.
Ordered list of numbers.
Search for vectors closest to a query vector.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Errors, Rate Limits, Content Filters, and Timeouts
concepts.firstPrinciples: Remote calls fail for ordinary reasons: auth, quota, overload, safety filters, network slowness, and malformed requests. Production inference code must treat failure as a normal branch, not an exception to reality.
concepts.definition: complete, embed, and get_model_info raise azure.core.exceptions.HttpResponseError for non-success status codes. 429 means rate limiting or quota pressure and should use retry with exponential backoff. Content filtering can stop or alter responses depending on service behavior. Timeouts should bound waiting.
concepts.analogy: Calling a model is like calling air traffic control. Sometimes the channel is busy, your badge is invalid, or the requested route is blocked. Good pilots retry politely, log the reason, and never pretend clearance was granted.
concepts.mentalModel: Catch HttpResponseError, branch on status_code, and keep retries limited. Respect Retry-After when present. For safety-filtered output, surface a safe fallback. For timeouts, retry idempotent requests or return a controlled error to the caller.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Call[Inference call] --> OK[Success]
  Call --> Err[HttpResponseError]
  Err --> Rate[429 rate limit]
  Err --> Auth[401 or 403 auth]
  Err --> Filter[Content filter]
  Rate --> Backoff[Retry with backoff]
  Auth --> FixRole[Check managed identity role]
  Filter --> Safe[Return safe fallback]

code.caption: Retry 429s with backoff and handle content-filter style failures.
code.content:
import os
import random
import time
from azure.core.exceptions import HttpResponseError, ServiceRequestError, ServiceResponseError
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import UserMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
    connection_timeout=10,
    read_timeout=60,
)

def complete_with_retry(prompt: str, attempts: int = 4) -> str:
    for attempt in range(attempts):
        try:
            response = client.complete(
                model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
                messages=[UserMessage(prompt)],
                max_tokens=120,
            )
            return response.choices[0].message.content
        except HttpResponseError as exc:
            if exc.status_code == 429 and attempt < attempts - 1:
                retry_after = exc.response.headers.get("Retry-After") if exc.response else None
                delay = float(retry_after) if retry_after else (2 ** attempt) + random.random()
                time.sleep(delay)
                continue
            if "content" in str(exc).lower() and "filter" in str(exc).lower():
                return "The request or response was blocked by the content filter. Please rephrase safely."
            raise
        except (ServiceRequestError, ServiceResponseError) as exc:
            if attempt == attempts - 1:
                raise
            time.sleep((2 ** attempt) + random.random())

print(complete_with_retry("Explain retry backoff in one sentence."))

drills.q: Which exception type should you catch for non-success HTTP responses?
drills.a: HttpResponseError.

drills.q: What should you do on 429?
drills.a: Retry with bounded exponential backoff and respect Retry-After when present.

drills.q: What is the safe behavior for content filter blocks?
drills.a: Do not force output; return a safe fallback or ask for a safer rephrase.

concepts.teachBack: Failures are part of inference engineering. Catch HttpResponseError, handle 429s politely, and bound network waits. Safety filters and auth errors need explicit user-safe branches, not blind retries.

glossary.definition: Azure Core exception for non-success HTTP status responses.
HTTP status commonly used for rate limiting.
Retry delay strategy that waits longer after repeated failures.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Async Inference
concepts.firstPrinciples: Synchronous code waits with its hands in its pockets. Async code can wait for the model while letting other requests progress. The model is still remote; async improves concurrency, not model intelligence.
concepts.definition: The async clients live in azure.ai.inference.aio, with equivalent methods such as complete and embed. Async code uses async with, await, and async iteration for streams. The docs note aiohttp is needed for asynchronous clients.
concepts.analogy: A synchronous cashier serves one customer at a time. An async cashier takes an order, hands it to the kitchen, and starts the next order while the first cooks.
concepts.mentalModel: Use async in web APIs, bots, and services that handle many simultaneous users. Use a single client per process or request scope as appropriate, close it cleanly, and do not block the event loop with time.sleep or CPU-heavy work.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  RequestA[Request A] --> AwaitA[Await model]
  RequestB[Request B] --> AwaitB[Await model]
  AwaitA --> ResponseA[Response A]
  AwaitB --> ResponseB[Response B]
  Loop[Event loop] --> AwaitA
  Loop --> AwaitB

code.caption: Async chat completion with managed identity.
code.content:
import asyncio
import os
from azure.identity.aio import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference.aio import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import UserMessage

async def main() -> None:
    async with DefaultAzureCredential() as credential:
        async with ChatCompletionsClient(
            endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
            credential=credential,
        ) as client:
            response = await client.complete(
                model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
                messages=[UserMessage("Explain async inference in one analogy.")],
                max_tokens=100,
            )
            print(response.choices[0].message.content)

asyncio.run(main())

drills.q: What namespace contains async inference clients?
drills.a: azure.ai.inference.aio.

drills.q: Does async make a single model response compute faster?
drills.a: Usually no. It lets your app handle other work while waiting.

drills.q: What Python keyword waits for async complete?
drills.a: await.

concepts.teachBack: Async inference is about concurrency. Use azure.ai.inference.aio and await remote calls. It is essential for scalable web apps and bots but still needs retries and timeouts.

glossary.definition: Programming style where waiting operations yield control to an event loop.
Azure SDK namespace suffix for asynchronous clients.
Runtime scheduler for async tasks.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

concepts.title: Model-Specific Extra Parameters
concepts.firstPrinciples: The common inference schema cannot know every special knob every model provider invents. Extra parameters are an escape hatch: useful, powerful, and dangerous if you forget they are model-specific.
concepts.definition: ChatCompletionsClient and EmbeddingsClient accept model_extras, a dictionary added as-is to the root JSON request body. The SDK docs state that when model_extras is included, the client automatically adds the extra-parameters pass-through header; service behavior depends on that header and model support.
concepts.analogy: Standard parameters are dashboard controls installed in every car. model_extras is a manufacturer-specific hidden switch. It may work beautifully on one vehicle and do nothing or fail on another.
concepts.mentalModel: Use model_extras only after reading the target model's docs. Keep it isolated in configuration, add tests, and log model/version. If portability matters, prefer standard parameters first.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Standard[Standard parameters] --> Body[Request body]
  Extras[model extras] --> Body
  Body --> Header[extra parameters pass through]
  Header --> Model[Provider specific handling]

code.caption: Pass provider-specific options separately from standard options.
code.content:
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import UserMessage

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

# Example only: replace these with parameters documented by your model provider.
provider_specific = {
    "safe_prompt": True,
    "custom_generation_mode": "balanced",
}

response = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=[UserMessage("Give one tip for portable inference code.")],
    temperature=0.3,
    model_extras=provider_specific,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

drills.q: Where are model_extras inserted?
drills.a: At the root of the JSON request body.

drills.q: Are model_extras portable across all models?
drills.a: No. They are model-specific.

drills.q: What does the SDK add when model_extras is present?
drills.a: The extra-parameters pass-through header, according to the docs.

concepts.teachBack: model_extras is the escape hatch for provider-specific knobs. Use standard parameters first. When you need extras, isolate them and document the exact model that supports them.

glossary.definition: Dictionary of model-specific request fields added to the root payload.
Service behavior that allows extra parameters through to the model.
Ability for code to work across models or providers.

sources.title: Azure AI Inference client library for Python

quiz.q: Which client is used for chat-style text generation in azure-ai-inference?
quiz.explain: ChatCompletionsClient calls chat completion models; EmbeddingsClient returns vectors.

quiz.q: Where is the first generated chat answer usually found?
quiz.explain: The first choice contains a ChatResponseMessage whose content holds the generated text.

options: It returns iterable updates with deltas
quiz.explain: Streaming returns partial updates as the model generates content.
max_tokens caps how many tokens the model may generate.

quiz.q: What is the correct raw function-calling loop?
options: Define tools, receive tool_calls, execute validated functions, send ToolMessage results
Let the model execute local Python directly
Send API keys in the tool schema
quiz.explain: The model requests tool calls; your code validates and executes them, then sends results back.

quiz.q: Which exception should code catch for non-success Azure SDK HTTP responses?
quiz.explain: azure.core.exceptions.HttpResponseError carries status_code, reason, and message.

quiz.q: What current AIProjectClient method is documented for an authenticated OpenAI-compatible client?
quiz.explain: Current azure-ai-projects docs list get_openai_client. The inference subclient method is not shown in the current public reference.
